desc:
This rough dirt road filled with assorted loose stones seems to be in a sad
state of repair. Though its ruts are shallow, its more numerous and much deeper
pits make the road very bumpy. Any wheeled vehicle would be in for quite a ride
as the road curves from west to south. Curiously, many tracks lead off the road
into a narrow ditch east of here that catches your eye, maybe because it is a
change in the bleak scenery of grassy plains that span out to the north.
name: On a Winding Roadway

desc:
Not much distinguishes this section of road from the rest of the packed dirt,
mixed stones and random pits that you see on the road that leads from Bree to
Tharbad. A rocky bank to the west of here obstructs any clear view of the lands
lying beyond in that direction, but to the south an expanse of fields and grassy
lowlands can be seen.
name: The Road to Tharbad

desc:
Yet again, the road changes its heading, now going west-south. From the north to
the south-east, a plain stretches seemingly unobstructed as far as the eye can
see, with the exception of a great forest occupying the lands several miles
north of here.
name: Turn in the Road

desc:
The old road gradually bends, evading the rough terrain to the west by taking a
northern heading. East of here, the road continues towards Tharbad while going
through vast plains. To the south the sound of rushing water can be heard as
steep cliffs plunge into the river far below.
name: Bend in the Road

desc:
Going west and south, the road slowly descends an elevation in the terrain,
eventually reaching the banks of the Nen Kalthar river some distance to the
south. Although not in prime condition, the quality of the road seems to have
improved somewhat compared to its state further north, suggesting that one might
be getting closer to its destination, the infamous city of Tharbad.
name: Road to Tharbad

desc:
Twisting and turning, the road struggles on. To the south a strip of lush
grassland lies between the road and the river, whilst to the west a light
woodland borders the thoroughfare. The countryside here is quite flat, and in
the distance to the southeast the walls of the city of Tharbad loom up above the
land.
name: Road to Tharbad

desc:
The old road goes on, along the crest of a long slope which gradually descends
to meet the churning waters of the Nen Kalthar south-west of here. The road
itself shows evidence of frequent use - the heavy wagons travelling along it
have left deep tracks, in some spots reducing the road into a muddy pool. Some
half-hearted attempts to repair the worst sections have been made, yet it seems
like the city is considered to be located too far from here to motivate any
major effort.
name: Road to Tharbad

desc:
The road bends in a north-east curve, following the lines of a steep and grassy
escarpment. To the west, the ground slopes down to the banks of the Nen Kalthar.
Frequent use has taken its toll on the road, yet apparently some effort has been
made at makeshift repairs, from time to time, as the thoroughfare remains good
enough for travel on foot, on horseback, or in a coach or wagon.
name: A Bend in the Road

desc:
A steep escarpment to the north forces the road to turn from south to west as it
makes its way between Tharbad and the far distant village of Bree. To the east,
you can see a vast area of marshland, and the ground slowly begins its slope
down towards the banks of the Nen Kalthar in the west.
name: Curve in the Road

desc:
The roads sweeps from the north to the east, turning away from the nearby river.
Loose cobblestones can be seen here and there, but the thoroughfare is in a
fairly good state of repair. To the south, a thick natural hedgerow of shrubs
and grasses borders the roadway, and offers a home to a variety of birds and
small creatures, who are heard more often than seen.
name: Road to Tharbad

desc:
The road here stands slightly above the surrounding land, raised on a small
causeway, presumably to protect it from floodwaters from the nearby rivers. At
the bottom of a small incline, a narrow path meets the road then heads north and
east, skirting the border of the marshes. To the south, the road approaches the
gates to the city of Tharbad.
name: On the Road to Tharbad

desc:
A steady flow of water splashes over the top of the dam to the west, kicking up
sediment, and making this part of the river quite murky. The shorelines to
either side of the waterway are steep and slick with mud, making these banks
extremely difficult to negotiate.
name: Below the Dam

desc:
The Nen Kalthar is slowly flowing to the south. A wooden dam, which can be seen
to the west, is stoping up the water and makes it calm and gentle. Steep shore-
lines prevent you from climbing out of the water, so the only exits are west
towards the dam, or to the south by following the river downstream.
name: The Nen Kalthar

desc:
There is a sharp turn in the waterway here, as the Nen Kalthar turns to the
east, from its previous southerly course. The riverbank to the south is quite
steep, but stony, and numerous tree roots can be seen poking through, which
might make it possible to climb to land, in the small woods to the south of the
river.
name: The Nen Kalthar

desc:
The turbulent waters of the river flow south from here, and the riverbanks to
the north and west are steep and muddy. Sweeping onward, the river makes its way
inexorably toward the mighty Greyflood, which can be seen to the southeast, near
to the looming walls of the city of Tharbad.
name: The Nen Kalthar

desc:
Strong currents often sweep any trespassers along, allowing little time to get a
glimpse of the surroundings. Woods stretch to the very border of the river to
the west, however, allowing a strong swimmer to reach the relative safety of
solid ground.
name: The Nen Kalthar

desc:
As the river cuts its way through the flat countryside, toward the south, its
banks become lower and gentler. Further upstream the banks become steep and
muddy as the land rises above the river. To the north, by climbing a low and
sandy bank, an area of lush grassland lies between the river and an old road. To
the east, the riverbank is topped by a thick, natural hedgerow so dense as to
prevent travel in that direction.
name: The Nen Kalthar

desc:
The current here is more intense than in the northern parts of the Nen Kalthar
since you are close to the river Greyflood. You see flat land to your west and
south. A little further downstream is the ancient city of Tharbad, but the area
around here does not seem very populous.
name: The Nen Kalthar

desc:
The river winds southward here through flat, muddy land, strewn with branches
and leafless trunks from earlier floods. To the east is the ancient city of
Tharbad, raised up on its mounds of debris and its old and rotting pilings.
name: The Nen Kalthar

desc:
The river winds south across a muddy floodplain. On the west bank are some
poorly drained but fertile enough fields. Eastward can be seen the ancient Port
of Tharbad, and the broad River Greyflood flowing unhurriedly toward the distant
sea.
name: The Nen Kalthar

desc:
Here the comparatively clear waters of the Nen Kalthar, flowing south from the
plains of Minhiriath, are absorbed by the great muddy torrent of the Gwathlo, or
Greyflood. Its water is murky, and, having taken unto itself the wastes of the
city of Tharbad just upstream, rather smelly as well; unpleasant lumps float
upon the flood. There are few fish here, and the banks are covered with debris.
name: Mixing of the Waters

desc:
The broad river forms a great, almost stagnant pool here as it struggles to
absorb the waters of the Nen Kalthar to the west. The water is not terribly
deep, but is extraordinarily dirty and not very healthy looking either. There
are some dead fish rotting along the banks.
name: The Greyflood

desc:
The broad, grey-brown river flows slowly toward the distant sea. The bank to the
south and east is low, muddy, but easily climbable. Occasionally a bird will
flap down from above and attempt to snatch a fish from the water. A few miles to
the north is the old city of Tharbad, of which so many stories have been told.
name: The Greyflood

desc:
There is not much happening here and it's quiet except for the lapping sound of
the water. Water is all around you, serving as a nice place for fish to dwell
in. However, you think you can see some land far northwest and southeast.
name: The Greyflood

desc:
The brown water of the Greyflood, pungent with the 'gifts' bestowed on it by the
population of Tharbad, flows slowly southwestward. Broad and slow here, miles
wide, it seems placid and unthreatening at the moment; but when it floods, it
must threaten disaster to anyone living or farming along its banks.
name: The Greyflood

desc:
Looking down in the water you see some fish, swimming fast away, frightened by
your presence. You see more of the river looking north, east and south. To the
west you can see some very high grass.
name: The Greyflood

desc:
In the river Greyflood, the water reflects the images you see before you like a
large blurry mirror. Looking north, west and south you can see more of this
mirror-like water. East there are some kind of plains.
name: The Greyflood

desc:
You must be in the middle of the river Gwathlo, as it is also called. Water is
in every direction you look. Peering down you see fish swimming in the clear
water. The current will soon take you south or west.
name: The Greyflood
The Greyflood

desc:
You're in the middle of the river Greyflood. In all directions you see water and
more water. However, looking around you, you can see something rising above the
surface to the northwest.
name: The Greyflood

desc:
This part of the Greyflood runs slowly past the southern bank. The shore is flat
and entangled with thick brush, reeds, and cattails. A few sunken bridges lie
rotting just below the surface in the reeds. The occasional splash of a fish
interrupts the solemn sound of the river flowing.
name: The Greyflood

desc:
The river flows south here below a steeply cut bank. Above it is a rocky hill or
pile of stones, whose bare surface can be seen above the tops of the foliage of
the steep shore. To the south the water foams downward over great boulders;
droplets of spray rise up, and there is a booming sound.
name: The Greyflood

desc:
This wide, grassy field sits by the bend in the road to Tharbad northeast of
here. Though some rocks have been kicked off the road, there are more stones
west of here than to the north or east. South the land becomes grassier, as it
draws closer to a small river.
name: Fields by the Road

desc:
The river bank here is quite high, and has been eroded and undercut by the flow
of the water, making it inadvisable to attempt to enter the water at this point.
At some distance both north and east, a wide road snakes its way across the
countryside, almost tracing the path of the nearby watercourse.
name: On a High River Bank

desc:
A sparse woodland of tall, slender trees rises to the west of here, and to the
east and north, a wide road sweeps by. The land here stands at some height above
the course of the Nen Kalthar, and the turbulent waters of the river flow from
west to south, at the bottom of a steep, muddy bank. Whilst slithering down the
bank to the water seems easy enough, returning to this point might prove to be
impossible.
name: Beside the Wide Road

desc:
The Nen Kalthar flows parallel to the road here. At the bottom of a steep and
muddy bank, the waters tumble past, but the river has left its mark here. The
land is lush, the grass green and thick, and flowers and shrubs grow in
abundance. The sound of songbirds and the splash of the river fill the air.
name: Fields by the River

desc:
A wide ribbon of grassland lies between the Road to Tharbad and the Nen Kalthar.
The waters of this small river have fed the land well, and the grass here grows
tall and healthy. Shrubs and small trees abound, forming a natural hedgerow,
which starts here, and winds beside the road to the east, and the waterway to
the south.
name: Between Road and River

desc:
The swirling waters of the Greyflood and the smaller river Nen Kalthar meet just
a little to the south of here, outside the imposing walls of the city of
Tharbad. The soil here is dark and rich, and a thick tangle of thorny bushes and
shrubs flourishes on these low banks, and nestles up against the rough stone
wall which can be seen to the east.
name: In a Thick Hedgerow at the Meeting of Two Waterways

desc:
Water flows lazily past north and east of here. You hear the soothing sound of
water splashing over the dam in the northwest. Both to the south and across the
river to the north, you see some sparse woodland. The light green grass here
seems to get less moisture than the healthier pasture in the west.
name: Fields by the River

desc:
Thin, but tall and healthy, the trees that grow here provide some shade for the
thin carpet of water-starved grass and various sorts of bushes to hide under.
Flanked to the south and west by a steep escarpment, the woods give shelter from
the winds on plains beyond the ridge. The woods continue eastward.
name: Scattered Woods

desc:
Tall trees grow all around, in a light, airy, scattered woodland. The trees are
healthy, and the undergrowth made up of lush, green vegetation. To both the
north and east a river sweeps by, supplying the woods with life-giving water,
and further east a wide road can be seen, cutting a swath across the lowlands,
and leading inexorably toward the city of Tharbad.
name: In the Sparse Woodland beside the River

desc:
From these plains you can watch the water flowing in the river to your north and
west. Southwest you barely discern a small building in the distance. Moving east
and south you will take you to some more plains.
name: Plains by the River

desc:
You are near some plains which force the water to make a turn and flow from the
north to west. A few insects have flown from the land and are now circling
around you, trying to find a place to land. You see plain land east and south.
name: The Greyflood

desc:
This is a low, flat-topped bluff whose steep sides overlook the Greyflood on the
east and south. On the west and north there are thick, tangled bushes, and the
surface is very uneven, composed of big blocks of grey stone with deep black
crevices between them. Shrubs and small trees growing out of the cracks have
covered the stones with a tangle of roots.
name: Stony Mound

desc:
Bushes grow very dense and thick here. They have small thorns which lightly
scratch your skin, but don't really hurt you. There are a lot of clusters of
some sort of berries hanging here and there. The bushes are so dense you can
only go to the plains north or east.
name: Thick Bushes

desc:
The grass has grown very high here. When you turn around you can clearly see
where you came from. Butterflies and other insects can be seen in the air. There
are less grassy plains around you, except to the south, where you see lots of
large bushes of some kind.
name: High Grass

desc:
The grass is thick and surrounds a few young trees. The ground here must be very
fertile, because the vegetation is dense, but still short. You see more plains
everywhere you look.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
What you see is clearly the remainders of what was once a forest. There are some
trees left which at the time the others were cut down probably were too small to
be of any use. You see more plains everywhere you look.
name: Cleared Land

desc:
Stumps, old and rotten, can be seen everywhere. Once this piece of land was a
part of the forest which can be seen to the north. You can see more plains with
stumps to your east and south.
name: Field of Stumps

desc:
The ground beneath your feet makes a squishy sound when you walk on it. It is
far from marshy, but still you see water here and there. You notice some rotten
stumps here and there, indicating that this was once a forest. You see more
plains west and south. To the north and east you see the reason to why the
ground is wet, the Nen Kalthar.
name: Wet Plains

desc:
This is a flat piece of land with old stumps here and there. Some small trees
grow here, left to stand while others had to fall long ago. You see more plains
in all directions.
name: Cleared Land

desc:
The thick grass here almost makes it feel like you're walking on pillows. This
is a peaceful place. The plains stretch out in all directions, but looking
further east you think you see something that looks like a riverbank.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
The grass is thick and soft. A cow could probably live all its life grazing on
these plains. The only animals you see here are not that big though. The river
to the east and south supplies the plains with fresh water. You see more plains
north, and west you see some thick bushes.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
The grass here is very high, housing many small animals and insects. You see a
river beyond the grass to the east and south. Less grassy plains can be seen
looking north and some very dense bushes are to the west.
name: High Grass

desc:
You are standing on the beach by the Greyflood. The soil here must be very
fertile, judging by the vegetation. Various plants grow here and there in the
thick grass. You see plains north and west and the river east and south.
name: Riverside

desc:
The land here is as flat as a board; the soil is lush and rich, with thick grass
and small shrubs growing wildly everywhere. Here and there are tilled fields,
but there is no sign of the houses of the farmers. Possibly they live in
Tharbad, across the river, working their fields by day, and at night retreating
into the dubious safety of the city's walls.
name: Plains by the River Junction

desc:
Before you stands a huge wooden gate with great spars of iron traversing it. You
have come before the southern gate of the City of Thieves, Tharbad. Who knows
what adventures will befall you once inside this famous city.
name: Outside the South Gate of Tharbad

desc:
This is an ancient stone road built atop a causeway which rises high above the
low and muddy landscape on either side. Here it bends from the east toward the
north, where a city wall can be seen in the distance.
name: The North-South Road

desc:
Here the road runs into an intersection. Scattered around it are several old
ruins. Perhaps remnants of the houses of those that once inhabited these lands.
There seem to have been several buildings, on both sides of the road. Maybe it
once was a small village. What seems to once have been a fountain of some kind
can still be seen at the south side of the road. Westward and southward the Old
South Road runs, while the ruins continue to the east along the Road of the
Smiths. The way north of here is blocked by the ruins of a fallen house.
name: Decaying Ruins

desc:
Here the road is broader and higher compared to other parts of the road. Mostly
due to the frequent flooding by the bordering swamps and the resulting muddy
residue. North of here are the swamps that are so typical for this area. West of
here the road becomes narrower while east of here some sort of ruins lie along
the road. South lie the remnants of what was once a cultivated area.
name: Old South Road

desc:
This is the road that will take you to the south gates of Tharbad. But it will
also take you far south if you follow it in that direction. The road is built on
a long causeway. From here you see the river Greyflood is to your north and you
see some marshy ground down the slope to your east.
name: Old South Road

desc:
The road goes north and east here. From the road you can travel straight west
without going down a slope and get to a small hill. Down the southern slope you
can barely notice a small trail leading down and in through the grassy field,
where it seems to disappear.
name: Old South Road

desc:
The road bends here and goes west and south, built on a causeway. What catches
your eye is a massive pile of dead trees to the east. It seems to block your way
to the Swanfleet swamp, but you also see swamp to the north.
name: Old South Road

desc:
The elevated road twists here and goes north to east. Down the slope to the
northeast there is a swamp named Nen-in-Eilph, but the view is blocked by black,
rotten, dead trees. You see plains to the south and some extraordinarily grassy
field to the west.
name: Old South Road

desc:
You're on the Old south road, leading from Tharbad in the northwest through the
Dunland in the south. The road will take you west or south here. There is a
swamp down a gentle slope to your east, but north some huge, black trees have
fallen and are blocking the way.
name: Old South Road

desc:
This is a bend in the Old South Road leading west and south. The road slopes
slightly on its northern and eastern sides, which border a swamp of some sort.
name: Old South Road

desc:
Here the Old South Road bends and goes from the east to the north. To the east
the road slopes downwards and levels out with the ground level. You get the
feeling that you're not too far away from a city, mostly because there is a
small tower visible to the northwest. South you only see plains.
name: Old South Road

desc:
The old broken road continues its way through the deserted land. Overgrown with
grass and littered with rubble, the road is in bad shape from lack of use.
Endless swamps stretch out northward and eastward across the flat landscape. Far
in the east faint outlines of a structure of some kind are barely visible. South
and west of here the road stretches on.
name: Old South Road

desc:
Severely cracked and full of holes and bumps, the dull road winds itself in a
north and easterly direction. A sort of thick brown weed is the only plantlife
visible. The border of the road is partly immersed in water that has overflowed
from the swamp area that lies just northeast of here. To the south and west the
land stretches out into barren plains.
name: Old South Road

desc:
Although old and decaying due to lack of use, the road here is still sturdy and
compact. Healthy vegetation crowds alongside and sometimes onto the old road.
North of here a swampy area stretches out. West and eastward the road continues
while south of here lies a large flat plain.
name: Old South Road

desc:
You are on some grassy plains which are bordered by the Old South Road to the
north and east. The plains continue south and west from here.
name: Plains by the Road

desc:
The grass seems to grow badly here. The ground does not seem to be able to
nourish the grass and the plants that once grew here. Looking north you can see
a high tower, but you see no entrance from this direction.
name: Dry Plains

desc:
Grass beneath your feet and grasshoppers jumping around you. Yes this is the
plains of the south. An enormous area of plains stretches out before you. It is
a very calm place you're in and the fresh wind gently fans your face.
name: Plains

desc:
A light breeze sets some tall blades swaying slowly. This is a very quiet and
tranquil place. The only thing which disturbs the silence is the sound of the
wind in your ears. When you look around you all you can see is plains in all
directions.
name: Tranquil Plains

desc:
This is a small trail leading north and south. The blades of the grass have been
bent at the ground, trampled. The trail is barely noticeable when you're just a
few feet off it. Someone is using this regularly, but for what and where does it
lead?
name: Trail in the Field

desc:
Grass and small plants grow around you. Walking through the grass you hear
something moving behind you. When you turn around you only see the furry tail of
some animal disappearing behind some small bush. Looking around you, you see
plains in all directions.
name: Plains

desc:
A few small animals are dashing from one small shrubbery to another on these
plains which lay by the side of some river you see to your west. In the other
directions you just see more plains.
name: Fields of Tharbad

desc:
You are by the riverside of the Greyflood, which is to your west and also to the
north. The water is gently flowing along the shore line south-west. To the east
and south you see plains stretching out before you.
name: A Shore

desc:
Walking on the small trail, made by trampling feet only, you see plains all
around you, except for a wedge-shaped hill to the east. You can't seem to climb
it from this direction, but the south side seems to have a nice slope.
name: Trail by the Hill

desc:
This is an odd-looking hill. Having its slope to the south and all other sides
of it unclimbable makes it look like a wedge. However, something that cannot be
seen from anywhere but here is a hole in the hill, leading down. Right by the
hole there are a few bones of some creature. The hill is surrounded by plains,
but there is a road to the north-east.
name: Wedge Shaped Hill

desc:
These plains are surrounded by a bending road to the north and east. Looking
west you see there's a hill that looks like a wedge, its slope in the southly
direction. Some more plains can be seen to the south.
name: Fields of Tharbad

desc:
The grass here is about a foot in height, and looking around you you see more
plains of this kind in all directions but east. There you see a thin, but quite
high tower. It looks like a watch tower of some kind. You can see no entrance on
this side, but a few black windows stare at you from the top.
name: Fields of Tharbad

desc:
Grass surrounds you. A few insects are humming around you as you notice there is
a slope to the north which could be a hill of some kind. South, north and west
you see more plains.
name: Plains near a Slope

desc:
Hidden in the grass is this trail. You figure it's been used not too long ago,
or the grass would have covered it rather quickly. There is a hill visible far
to the north-east. Otherwise the only thing you see around you is plains.
name: Trail by the Hill

desc:
This trail is barely noticeable in the grass. Apparently it's not being used too
often. The trail makes a bend here, leading south from the east. A small hill
can be seen to the north, while you see a shore of a river to the west.
name: Trail in the Plains

desc:
From here you can see the city of Tharbad not far north of here. You also see
water when you look down the hill to the west. You see a road to the east and to
the south you see a plain with a small trail cutting through it.
name: Small Hill

desc:
The trail is almost hidden in the high grass. It's not even a trail, it looks
more like the tall grass is bent in a certain direction, indicating someone has
been walking here. The Old South Road can be seen here to the north and east. To
the south you can see a high hill which is too steep to climb. The trail
continues westwards from here.
name: Trail in the Plains

desc:
Once a large burial chamber, this place holds several mansized caskets. The
caskets are opened and mostly emptied nowadays, their human remains scattered
all about the room. Whether this has been done by looters or animals is hard to
tell. Bones lie everywhere in disarray, disarticulated and gnawed. In the middle
of the room the floor has collapsed, giving way for a great dark crevice.
name: Ruined Crypt

desc:
Sitting along the road here are the remnants of buildings long ago abandoned.
One of the ruins seems to stand out among the rest. It is the largest of the
structures and has not decayed as much as the others. The only exit you can see
is through a large arch. Westwards the ruins continue along the road, while
eastward the Road of the Smiths continues.
name: Decaying Ruins

desc:
The road begins to narrow as it continues on towards the east. Old ruins
intercept the road westwards. Crickets can be heard chirping in the fields to
the south. The ground to the north is wet and muddy, indicating an area of
swamps may lead ahead in that direction.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
Through half erect arches a large room becomes visible. The room is filled with
litter and bricks from the walls and roof that once stood here. The only sign of
any activity around here are some animal tracks on the floor and some bird nests
on top of the walls. In the northeast corner lies a decayed stone altar covered
with dirt. The only exit out of here seems to be an arch leading south.
name: Inside the Ruined Building

desc:
Here the swamp borders the side of an old road. The soft ground and numerous
surrounding large muddy holes makes it a bit difficult to move about. The
passage east of here is blocked by the ruins of what once was a large stone
building. Westward the swampy area continues while north of here is the bank of
a mud filled lake.
name: Outskirts of the Swamp

desc:
Here the outskirts of a gloomy swamp border the side of an old road. Large muddy
holes and soft ground make for difficult passage for travellers. North and
northeast of here awaits the swamp with all its secrets. Far northeast, the
faint outlines of something can be seen rising toward the skies.
name: Outskirts of the Swamp

desc:
Here the pools of dark murky water are sparse and thin. The area is mostly
covered with a thick layer of brownish weed and magnolia bushes. A few old
branchy pine trees hovers precariously with their exposed knotty roots by the
murky pools. South, north and west of here the swamp continues while eastward it
is blocked by a mud-filled lake.
name: In the Swamps

desc:
The outskirts of the swamp loom quietly on the northern border of the Old South
Road. The swampy area seems to stretch out endlessly in the northeastern and
northwestern directions. Large muddy pools of dark, murky water forewarns of
danger and difficult passage for the daring travellers. The Old South Road
continues to the south.
name: Outskirts of the Swamp

desc:
Here the swamp consists more or less of pure mud. Some brown weed grows here in
squarelike formations, giving the impression of borders and boundaries in this
formless swamp. There seems to be no other plant life around. The swamp con-
tinues in all directions.
name: In the Swamps

desc:
Here deep within the swamp there are no signs of paths or passages in any form.
Large dark pools of mud converge and diverge wherever the curvature of land
leads them. The vegetation consists mostly of a thick brownish weed and some
heather. Far to the north a river winds its way toward the sea, while to the
northeast a building of some sort rises toward the skies. The swamp continues in
all directions.
name: In the Swamps

desc:
This is an edge of the swamp. You can see a road that has to be the Old South
Road to your west and south. Moving north or east here will take you deeper into
the Nen-in-Eilph.
name: The Swanfleet Swamp

desc:
Spreading out in all directions the slimy mud seem to cover the whole swampy
area. The only vegetation around here is some sturdy flooding swamp weed and one
or two sickly pine trees with their crippled branches. North, east and west of
here the swamp continues with its endlessness.
name: In the Swamps

desc:
Due to all the pools and mudholes around here it is not always easy to find a
safe path around. The thick brown weeds that dominate this area cover all the
rest of the vacant land. East of here the weeds seem to become thin and more
sparse, while north and west of here the swamp continues. South of here the
passage is blocked by a large muddy pool.
name: In the Swamps

desc:
Here the vegetation is almost lifeless. Even the weeds are sickly and limp. The
air hangs thick with a foul repelling odor originating from the bubbling pools
that lie scattered here. To the south the vegetation seems to be more vibrant
while north of here the lifeless area continues. The swamp stretches to the east
and west of here.
name: In the Swamps

desc:
Not too overflooded with water the vegetation here is a bit healthier and
colourful. Beside the brown weed and the crippled trees the are here is also
covered by heather and some blueberries. South of here faint outlines of some
buildings can be seen, but the way is blocked by a lake of bubbling mud. The
same lake also blocks the passage westward. The swamp continues to the north and
east.
name: In the Swamps

desc:
Large, muddy pools dotted with tufts of heather mark the beginning of the swampy
area that lies north of here. Westward the passage is blocked by an old moss
covered stone wall. North of here the swamp continues on, while to the east and
south you can see an old road.
name: Outskirts of the Swamp

desc:
The region is filled with pools of different sizes. Little appears to grow here,
except for the thick weeds common throughout the swamp and a few crippled pine
trees. Occasionally, a small animal can be heard off in the distance. The pools
drain slowly into a larger pool towards the east. This pool looks stagnant and
unsafe. The swamp continues on in all directions.
name: In the Swamps

desc:
The thick, choking vegetation and soft, wet ground makes the journey here very
difficult. A great host of insects gives the air a strange, hazy look to it. The
swamp continues on in all directions, the sun and moon the only recogniz- able
landmarks for directions in this desolate area.
name: In the Swamps

desc:
Black, huge, rotten trees surround you. They look like skeletons of large
creatures as they have no leaves, but are still dense enough to keep the sun-
light out. Some trees have fallen and are now decaying on the ground. A cold
shiver runs down your spine as you feel you are being watched. You cannot see
anything special, but you hear a faint gurgling sound.
name: Black Grove

desc:
More swamp. You see swamp north, east and south. Looking west you can see some
dark trees which leave you staring at them, thinking of what's there. Strangely
enough there are very few insects here. Something seems to keep them away from
this place. Whatever it is it probably isn't dangerous.
name: The Swanfleet Swamp

desc:
The ground here is marshy and swampy. Insects already start to irritate you
although you're just on the edge of this swamp, called the Swanfleet. The swamp
stretches out north and east. You see a road to the west and south.
name: Edge of the Swamp

desc:
The humidity is high here and you can feel it when you breathe. Mosquitoes are
swarming around you and your feet stick to the marshy ground. Everywhere you
look you see nothing but swamp. However, looking south you see a clump of trees
in the marsh.
name: The Swanfleet Swamp

desc:
Your skin is sticky from the high humidity. As you smack another mosquito its
remains smeared on your skin and it takes great effort to get rid of it. The
swamp surrounds you, leaving you all directions available for travel.
name: The Swanfleet Swamp

desc:
The ground here gives the illusion of firmness and solidity. One wrong step in
the wrong direction however, will land unsuspecting travellers head over heels
into slushy muddy pools. Dense floating vegetation provides camouflage for the
wicked awaiting pools. North of here lies the river Glanduin, while the swamp
continues to the north, south, and west.
name: In the Swamps

desc:
Here amidst all the mud and water lies a tiny island of rock. Surrounded on all
sides by the slimy green of the lurking swamp, the island's rocky surface shows
a surprising red-brownish tint. An indication to the possible iron ore contents
of the island perhaps. The centre of the island shows faint traces of a forge
that once was here. From here the swamp continues in all directions.
name: In the Swamps

desc:
Most of the swamp here is overgrown with dense thickets consisting of large
thornbushes. Thinly spread out are some crippled pine trees and some brownish
looking weed. North of here the thicket becomes more dense and ends at the shore
of a dark pool of mud. The swamp continues to the east, west and south.
name: In the Swamps

desc:
Thick wet mud covers the ground here. Some small bushes can be seen in small
lumps here and there. Clouds of mosquitoes hover above the bushes for prolonged
periods of time, forming ever-changing shapeless crown of grey over the plants.
The vegetation becomes more dense to the north and the bushes grow larger and
thicker. The swamp continues to the west, east and south.
name: In the Swamps

desc:
A few crippled pine trees grow here among thick growth of a thick brown weed of
some sort. The weed seems to be the main inhabitant here and covers most of the
region. To the east the weeds thin out and take on a yellowish looking colour.
North of here the swamp runs into the river while south, east and west the swamp
continues.
name: In the Swamps

desc:
The only sound heard around here is of running water. Otherwise the area is more
or less dead. Thick bunches of half-rotten weed emit an unmistakeable smell. To
the south of here the lifeless area continues while to the east and west the
swamp continues. To the north of here the swamp runs into the river Glanduin.
name: In the Swamps

desc:
The swampland here appears to have been split between the barren ground to the
west and the numerous bushes to the east. Scattered among the weeds are small
tufts of heather. A bit to the northeast you can spy a large tower. The river
Glanduin can be heard to the north. The swamp continues on to the south.
name: In the Swamps

desc:
Here the swampy area merges with some wetlands to the east. Soft mud melts
together with earth and water making the grounds treacherous. The bubbling of
the Glanduin can be heard to the north, while the swamp expands to the south and
west.
name: Swamp along the River

desc:
The swamps bordering the river here make for a very poor riverbank. Soft mud
parts and gives away to the slightest pressure. South of here the swamp grows
denser while the muddy riverbank continues to the west and east along the river.
North of here the river flows by complacently.
name: Swamp along the River

desc:
The swamp here borders a fast running river. Soft wet mud serves as a bank for
the river and a border between the two bodies. Continuing southwards the swamp
grows more dense with reeds and bushes of various kinds. Eastwards the sparsly
covered and muddy riverbank continues. North of here the river rapidly flows
past while a dense thorny thicket grows to the west.
name: Swamp along the River

desc:
Here the thorn-bush becomes an unlikely sanctuary from the wet soggy grounds of
the swamp. Despite prickly thorny branches, the ground is at least solid and
dry. North and south of here natural passages through the thicket lead back into
the swamp. West of here the thicket becomes too thick and forms a deadend while
east of here the bush thins out.
name: Thorny Thicket

desc:
The river is bordered to the south by a somewhat elevated road which leads to
Tharbad's south gates. A swamp, taking over where the Sirannon splits up to join
the Greyflood can be seen to the east. North and west you see the water of the
river flow southwest towards Tharbad.
name: The Greyflood

desc:
One stage of its long journey to the coast completed, the Greyflood has reached
the walls of the city of Tharbad. The wide and majestic river carries vast
quantities of through the land each day, and as it travels through the city it
presents an ever-present threat of flood and destruction. Yet also, it is this
river which gave the city its life.
name: On a River Drawing close to Tharbad

desc:
The watercourse is particularly wide here, with a large area of shallows to
east, and extremely low shores to either side. Clear and sparkling, the waters
of the Glanduin and Hoarwell, combined and known now as the Greyflood, amble
sluggishly through the lowlands. Frequently breaching its banks, the river has
created an expanse of muddy fens, which extend much of the way around the walls
of the city of Tharbad.
name: On the Mighty River Greyflood

desc:
Flowing steadily southwest, the wide river makes its way through the marshes and
wetlands of the river basin. Though the waters seem quite torpid here, it would
be foolish to ignore their potential, for this is the very agent which has so
often wreaked havoc on the city of Tharbad, with angry floods, which tear at the
very foundations of the city.
name: The Greyflood

desc:
The River Hoarwell continues westward, joined by the Glanduin from the east, and
the combined waters flow on toward Tharbad. All about, the flat countryside has
been flooded often by the seasonal rise and fall of the waters of these two
mighty rivers, and now they find themselves travelling through a wide area of
marshes, as they continue their journey to the distant coast at Lond Daer.
name: At a Meeting of Two Waterways

desc:
The Greyflood flows to the south here, but it is apparent that it has often
broken its banks and flooded the ground to the west, feeding the broad expanse
of marshland beyond. Though still sluggish, except in times of flood, the waters
seem to gain a little life here, creating a light mist and patches of snow-white
foam atop the silvery surface as the river travels over an area of massive,
rounded boulders which rest in its path.
name: On a River amid the Marshland

desc:
The river meanders onward through the wetlands. Though the river is wide and
slow-flowing, it is also fairly shallow here, and white foam dances amid the
clear blue water as it splashes against large rocks on the riverbed. Flowing
from the north, the water courses westward from here, making its way towards the
city of Tharbad.
name: On a Meandering Waterway

desc:
Here the waters of the River Mitheithel flow westwards, where they meet the
River Greyflood to continue their journey southward towards the sea, past the
city of Tharbad.
name: River Mitheithel

desc:
The river here is broad and shallow as it winds its way throught the flat
countryside. Its low banks appear to have been breached often by the waters, and
you can see an area of marshland extending to the north and west. To the east
and south, you can see a wide delta formed by the waters of the River Glanduin.
name: The Widening Waters of the River Hoarwell

desc:
Looking north you see a simple wooden bridge, which becomes smaller to the eye
as the current carries you southward. To the southeast lies a great delta,
mingled with a peaceful, wildlife-laden marsh. The waters swirl slowly as the
swift Glanduin river intersects the proud path of the Mitheithel. West of here
these great waterways merge to become the Greyflood.
name: Delta of Glanduin

desc:
The river's current becomes less sure of itself as it nears a delta, where the
water flows slowly, if at all. To the north is a grassy peninsula, leading up to
a strong wooden bridge.
name: Glanduin Delta

desc:
The patches of grass rise from the water. The sound of small animals who live in
the marshy surroundings of the delta reaches your ears. To the south a small
marshy island rises from the crystal water.
name: Glanduin Delta

desc:
You see the heart of the Glanduin delta, surrounded by swirling water in all
directions but east. Far to the southwest the Glanduin and Mitheithel come
together to form the mighty Gwathlo River, beyond which it is rumoured the proud
towers of Tharbad loom, a shadow of evil in this otherwise peaceful environment.
name: Glanduin Delta

desc:
The Glanduin continues its way to the west. The river can be seen in all
directions but east, where marshes border the river. The delicate foliage of the
Swanfleet lies to the east.
name: Delta of Glanduin

desc:
The Glanduin flows into the Mitheithel here, their currents joining and
propelling various debris and wetland wildlife southward to the Gwathlo.
Well-tended fields far to the west stand out against the gentle marshes and
grasslands that border the river.
name: Delta of Glanduin

desc:
Small patches of green grass rise from the water surface. The water doesn't flow
very rapidly here, in fact it is almost still. Its brown, ugly colour makes it
very unattractive.
name: Delta of Glanduin

desc:
The water flows steadily form the north and east, running ever onwards to the
city of Tharbad in the distance. Carrying all manner of debris and water-life
with it the water looks slightly brown and murky here, but that is no doubt due
to almost stagnant water from the swamps in this area joining the flow.
name: Glanduin Delta

desc:
Surrounded to the north and east by the Delta of Glanduin the land is soft and
spongy. Examining the ground closer reveals many tracks from small animals. One
would have to move carefully here not to leave any tracks, if that is possible
at all.
name: Marsh

desc:
The current in this part of the river remains straight and calm, unaffected by
the swirling waters of the vast delta to the north-east. To the north is a patch
of shallow water.
name: Glanduin Delta

desc:
Flowing closer to the Greyflood the Glanduin gets wider and wider. The air is
still and quiet, punctuated only by an occasional low howl from the marshes to
the north.
name: Glanduin Delta

desc:
The crystal clear water of the river Glanduin has formed almost a pool here, by
its still current in this part of the delta. A marsh is to the north, its
delicate foliage swaying slightly in the breeze.
name: Glanduin Delta

desc:
In all directions the waters of the delta lie before you, and the sound of
flowing water seems to get stronger to the south.
name: The Glanduin

desc:
The air is filled with the sounds of life as many species of birds fly about,
attracted to the area by the abundant food and opportunity for nesting. To the
west peaceful marshes open before you, their delicate foliage shining in the
sun.
name: The Glanduin

desc:
Here the Glanduin continues on its way from east to west. The dark muddy water
flows past with steady speed. There are a few landmarks that manage to break the
horizon of this otherwise flat area. To the north lies the faint slope of a
large hill and east of here a tower rises above the swamp overlooking the river.
East and west of here the river continues while south of here lies the outskirt
of a large swamp.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
Here the river runs at a slow lazy pace, making for a safe spot for crossing.
But there is no immediate dry land in all directions. North and south of here
the swamps of this region stretch out and there are no signs of humanoid life.
East and west of here the river continues. Somewhat far to the north the slope
of a large hill is barely visible while southeast of here a tall building of
some sort rises above the swamp.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
Here the river makes a turn and continues its passage northwards. Even if the
current is not very strong here the water flows rapidly. The water is dark
because of the large amount of mud influx from the swamps into the river. East
of here the river continues while south and west are the outskirts of a swampy
delta.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
Sprouting from the cold, clean waters of the Glanduin, the grassy foliage around
you ranges in shade from green to golden. It sways gently in the shallow water,
providing food and cover for many varieties of wetland creatures. The elegant
marsh spreads westward, surrounded in all other directions by the depths of the
river.
name: Marsh

desc:
Here is an island of shallow wetlands that rises from the clear surface of the
Glanduin supporting an abundance of plant and animal life. To the south and
north from here the river encounters a marshy delta, where the waters of the
Glanduin strengthen its current and join it for the final trip to the sea.
name: Marsh

desc:
Rising above the water of the Glanduin Delta, this piece of land is covered with
short green grass. The grass appears trampled in certain places, perhaps by some
of the fishermen from Tharbad, who occasionally use these waters to make a
living.
name: Marsh

desc:
Here the ground is extremely soft and saturated. East of here the river takes a
sharp northward turn, constantly flooding the delta with rapid overflowing
currents. South of here is a large and dense thicket of thorny bushes. West and
north of here the swamp continues.
name: In the Swamps

desc:
Here the ground is more solid than other parts of the swamp. This has caused the
topsoil to be more fertile. The main inhabitant here is some short sturdy wild
grass. Dotted around this green layer are a few crippled pine trees and some
short magnolia bushes. North of here the swamp enters the river Glanduin while
east and west the swamp continues. South of here the way is blocked by a muddy
pool.
name: In the Swamps

desc:
Here the swamp is rather wet. Large muddy pools cover almost everything north
towards the river, while south and west of here the ground looks more dry. The
numerous deep pools make it difficult to find safe passage but there should be
enough enough dry land in all directions for a foothold. East of here the wet
area seems to end, while north of here the river Glanduin passes by. West of
here the swamp continues.
name: In the Swamps

desc:
Looking north, south, east and west you see nothing but swamp. The air is a bit
humid and has the odd, sour smell that only swamps have. You can hear the
flapping of wings nearby, but you also hear some strange sounds that definitely
doesn't sound like swans.
name: The Swanfleet Swamp

desc:
This is the Nen-in-Eilph, where numerous swans dwell. You see swamp in all
directions, but you can tell by the low humidity in the air that you're near the
edge of the swamp.
name: The Swanfleet Swamp

desc:
This is the western edge of the Wasteland of the Swans. You see the large swamp
before you and you can hear the distant sound of swans calling. The swamp lays
to the south and east. You see the flowing water of the river Greyflood to your
north and west.
name: West Edge of the Swanfleet Swamp

desc:
As the river flows languidly by, its waters lap gently over this patch of rocky
ground. The mud of marshland gives way here, washed clean by the persistent
waters, and a clear, stony riverbed is revealed in these shallows. Further west,
the watercourse becomes too deep to ford, though the current remains unhurried.
name: The Shallows of the River

desc:
The terrain here is extremely muddy, but does not have the same unhealthy
feeling as do the marshes northwest of the Greyflood, perhaps due to the fact
that here the ground is washed and renewed by the wandering waters of the
Glanduin, which form a massive delta here, as they cross the flat land so near
to the city of Tharbad.
name: Deep Mud

desc:
The river Glanduin continues to spread out here as it mingles with the great
Mitheithel. To the north and south the clear, cold water flows through delicate
grass and wetland foliage. The sound of wildlife is also loudest in those two
directions, although many birds fly above the river unthreatened by your
presence.
name: Glanduin Delta

desc:
The clear, cold waters of the Glanduin flow slowly amongst islands of golden
grass, supporting a diverse collection of wildlife. To the east a grassy
riverbank, spotted with a few young trees, shows evidence of drier ground. To
the north the elegant Swanfleet marshes rise gently from the clean water, and to
the west the river continues its leisurely journey.
name: The Glanduin

desc:
The river now flows swiftly against the banks, pulling you towards a worn wooden
bridge directly to the south. What lies beneath the structure is hidden in
shadows, and remains a mystery to the curious eye. In the distance to the east
you can make out the form of a small wooden shack.
name: The River Hoarwell

desc:
To the south-west, across the vast fields, is the city of Tharbad. Although its
grey-stone walls are not visible from this distance, the well-tended fields to
the west serve as a reminder of its existence. To the north the fields are green
and lush, having a plentiful supply of water from the river Hoarwell. The
current continues in a southerly direction.
name: The River Hoarwell

desc:
The river surrounds you in almost every direction, moving westwards at a slow,
deliberate pace. The current quickens slightly as the river narrows and
approaches a simple wooden bridge in the distance.
name: The River Hoarwell

desc:
The wide Hoarwell continues on its southward course, its lazy waters flowing by
the grassy wetlands in the west. Further along the river seems to narrow, its
pace increasing slightly. In the distance you can see a simple wooden bridge
spanning from one bank to the other.
name: The River Hoarwell

desc:
Flowing gently to the southwest the Hoarwell river extends into the murky lands
towards Tharbad.
name: The River Hoarwell

desc:
The calm waters of the Hoarwell bend to the west as they flow against the grassy
riverbanks to the east and south. Peering eastward, you are confronted with
dense, fertile wetlands as far as the eye can see.
name: The River Hoarwell

desc:
The current seems to change direction here, as the Hoarwell wraps around a
grassy riverbank and flows to the southwest. To the north lush wetlands emerge
from the calm waters and spread westward.
name: The River Hoarwell

desc:
Bordered by the big river to the south and east, and a small stream to the west,
this piece of land is drenched with water. Each time you take a step you have to
struggle to lift your foot from the sucking ground. This would probably not be a
good place to replenish your water supply.
name: Soft Grounds

desc:
As it flows southwards, the river widens significantly. To the west the calm
waters lap peacefully against the grassy riverbank. In all other directions you
are surrounded by the slow, deliberate current of the Hoarwell. This
insignificant waterway is actually a small branch from the powerful Hoarwell
river, which can be heard to the south.
name: A Small Stream

desc:
The stream banks here are covered with green moss and large brownish fungi.
There is a rotting stench in the air and not many sounds can be heard.
name: A Smelly Stream

desc:
Faint shadows of marine life swimming underwater are visible here. The riverbank
to the south is quite damp and seems to suffer from frequent flooding. To the
east grassy fields roll up to a small crest, and to the north and west the river
continues.
name: The River Hoarwell

desc:
The calm waters of the Hoarwell seem to be in no hurry as they meander south-
wards, rolling by the lush green grass to the west. To the east the river grows
wider, and in the distance you can spot a beautiful, secluded riverbank.
name: The River Hoarwell

desc:
You see a grassy peninsula, extending into the waters of the Hoarwell. If you
head west, you will shortly cross a worn wooden bridge and approach the fields
surrounding Tharbad. To the east a soggy path leads away, to the north of which
sits a weather-beaten shack.
name: Before a Wooden Bridge

desc:
The trail of spongy grass leads in two directions here. Westward lies a simple
wooden bridge, and eastward the path approaches some thick marshes and a round
strangely overgrown hill. North of the trail you see a worn shack perched on the
grassy riverbank.
name: Wet Path

desc:
The path continues in an east - west direction, surrounded by tall reeds and
grass. The ground off the path looks slightly boggy, and straying from the
marked way may not be a good idea. To the South lies the delta of the great
Glanduin river.
name: Wet Path

desc:
The ground here is wet and spongy, covered with thick, green grass. To the north
you can see another path leading somewhere, surrounded by grassy wetlands. To
the south an elegant marsh rises from the waters teeming with wildlife.
name: Wet Path

desc:
You cannot help but feel relieved as you step out of the overgrown marsh foliage
and the trail opens to reveal the Mitheithel to the southwest and a strange hill
to the southeast. The ground here is still moist, but the grass is shorter and
less threatening than that of the dense swamp to the north. A waterlogged path
leads west, and you see a grassy riverbank to the south. To the east the slope
steepens, leading to drier ground.
name: Junction of Paths

desc:
The path runs north-south here, still delving deeply into the thick reeds and
muddy ground. The smell of stagnant mud and decaying plant life intensifies as
each step uncovers more new pockets of gas.
name: Path through the Wetlands

desc:
The path continues deeper into the marsh, bending south and west. Tall reeds
sway overhead in the breeze, making the dimly lit trail seem like a damp tunnel
through the tangled foliage. The winding path defies sense of direction.
name: Path through the Wetlands

desc:
A barely visible path here is leading from the shack in the east to the great
river in the west. It is surrounded by different kinds of marshy plants and wet
ground. It was probably used by some old fisherman who was living in the shack.
name: Small Path to the River

desc:
The shack is not as roomy as it appeared from the outside. Tools clutter the
walls and floor, most of which were used for fishing and fieldwork. A small
window in the western wall provides a good view of the Hoarwell as it pounds its
course to the sea. Benches and shelves covered with loose grain occupy the
remaining area, evidence of a time when this area was perhaps drier, and used
for farming.
name: Small Wooden Shack

desc:
The path leads to the old wooden shack rising out of the ocean of green grass
and reeds in the west. To the north you can see a grassy riverbank. The path
continues its way to the east.
name: Path to the Shack

desc:
The path leading to the shack in the west joins here a spongy path to the south.
In another time, this must have been a frequently used path, but somehow it came
to be abandoned.
name: Bend in the Path

desc:
Various species of marsh grass thrive here, fed by the excessive moisture and
rich silt deposits. To the north the grassy riverbank continues, and to the
south the density of the marsh seems to intensify. To the east your path is
blocked by thick, tangled reeds and dangerous looking mud pits.
name: Grassy Riverbank

desc:
The damp ground here is covered with lush grass, due to the substantial deposits
of silt left behind when the Hoarwell exceeds its bounds. To the north the river
flows steadily by, feeding the surrounding marshes with a constant supply of
moisture.
name: Grassy Riverbank

desc:
The waters to the north and west are bordered by grassy fields here, making this
almost a marshland. Directly to the south an old wooden shack sits on the bank
of the river, weathered with age.
name: Grassy Riverbank

desc:
Parallelling the river, the ground here is moist and fertile, sprouting many
grasses and small flowers. To the east and south the vast wetlands spread before
you, and to the north and west the Hoarwell flows peacefully by.
name: Grassy Riverbank

desc:
The ground is moist and fertile here, supporting many species of wetland fauna.
To the west the lazy waters of the Hoarwell lap calmly against the grassy
riverbank. To the north the grassy field continues onto higher ground. A small
path has been trampled through the marshes directly to your east.
name: Grassy Wetlands

desc:
The ground here is surprisingly dry, considering it is bordered by the river
Hoarwell to the west and dense wetlands to the south. Short grass grows here
making your way easier.
name: Fields by the River

desc:
The lazy waters of the Hoarwell flow peacefully by this secluded hollow, hidden
partially by a slight crest to the south. Several ancient, majestic trees grow
here, their limbs stretching out over the wide river, forming a perfect place
for the weary traveller to rest. To the east a small path leads away.
name: Secluded Riverbank

desc:
The ground here is moist, causing your feet to sink slightly with each step
through the swaying green foliage. To the east and south lies the River
Hoarwell, its calm waters lapping at their muddy riverbanks. To the west the
grassy fields seem to be somewhat drier.
name: Grassy Wetlands

desc:
The grass here is lush and healthy, fed with a constant supply of silt and
moisture. The sounds of sparkling water of the majestic Hoarwell is noticeable
in the distance. To the east the grassy plains continue seemingly without end.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
Each step leaves a rapidly diminishing mark in the soft and spongy ground.
Sounds from frogs and other swamp loving creatures reach your ears. It is quite
an effort walking at a pace here.
name: Soft Grounds

desc:
Thick green grass grows in a patchwork, interspersed with coarse sedge. Fed by
the waters of the river Hoarwell to the east of here, the land is rich, the soil
thick and loamy. Westward it seems that the wetlands begin to recede, but to the
east and south they stretch out before you.
name: Grassy Wetlands

desc:
Thick green grass, heather and tall sedges all grow here, seeming to battle one
another for dominance in this wetland area. To the north, the more lush grass
seems triumphant, but further south the land seems to become a little more
marshy, and the heather is more dominant. To the west, a tall wall of old,
weathered stones blocks the way, and marks the boundary of a farm whose large
barn can be seen across the fields to the southwest.
name: Grassy Wetlands

desc:
Closer to the city these wetlands show evidence of frequent use. Contrary to
popular belief, the entire city is not of a cut-throat nature and some of its
citizens feel more comfortable trying to survive on the land rather than stolen
goods and plunder. This is evident by the remains of a tattered fishing net that
lies at your feet.
name: Soft Grounds

desc:
As the river Hoarwell flows southwest across the land, the ground begins to
surrender to its influence, becoming peaty and marshy. Soft heather and tall
sedges thrive here and dominate the landscape. A tall stone wall seems to have
fallen into disrepair in the west, and a wide breach now gives access to the
fields and farm beyond.
name: At a Breach in the Wall

desc:
On the edge of the marshland, long deep cuttings can be seen scarring the
landscape. However, it appears that these are no recent work, as rough sedge has
already covered the vertical walls of the trenches. Not far to the west stands a
rather dilapidated stone building, and to the south a narrow trail can be seen
picking its way through the mire toward an old wooden bridge. To the east, the
fens are bordered by a wide river.
name: The Peat Cuttings

desc:
The faltering path branches north into the peaty marshland. This narrow trail is
almost non-existent, but the track looks more often used where it leads to a
small wooden bridge that spans the Hoarwell to the east. The waters flow past
lazily, but the broad expanse of boggy ground suggests that sometimes nature
rules as the waters breach the banks and flood the land beyond.
name: Trail by the Bridge

desc:
A model of functionality, this simple bridge is constructed of several giant
weathered timbers lashed together with thick rope and covered with sturdy
planks. Although weathered with age, it appears able to support a fair weight.
To the east lies a damp path through the spongy grass and to the west you see
vast fields, carefully tended by an unknown hand.
name: Bridge across the Mitheithel

desc:
The soft green grass that flourishes to the west of here gradually gives way to
coarser patches of sedge. To the south an old stone wall can be seen, separating
this grassland from the fields beyond. Tall, and made only from cleverly
interlocking boulders, the wall is surprisingly intact, and the many patches of
soft, green moss make it too slick to scale.
name: Grassy Wetlands

desc:
A short distance to the west a small, scattered woodland can be seen, its tall
trees offering some shelter from the winds which can howl across the flat plains
which dominate the countryside here. Small shrubs have sprung up here, fed by
the wetlands to the east, and the ground is made soft by grasses, heather and
small clumps of bracken. To the south an old stone wall apparently marks the
margin of some private land.
name: At the Eaves of a Small Woodland

desc:
A yellow and dying field stretches out before you. The green grass, and healthy
crops which once grew here were apparently destroyed by blight several seasons
ago, and no seed has been planted to replenish the land. Small holes can be seen
in the dirt, the hard labour of some unseen animal, but it is apparent that no
other has toiled here in a long time. Immediately to the south, a large wooden
barn looms up before you.
name: A Field before the Barn

desc:
Bounded on the east and north by a tall wall of dull grey stones, this land may
once have been a rich and fertile pasture. Now, however it stands spoiled.
Seasons of alternating rain, frost, and summer sun have transformed the ground
into a rock-hard crust, useless for any form of farming, and even the most
tenacious plants seem unable to gain a footing here.
name: Ruined Fields

desc:
A wide breach in the grey stone wall offers access to the marshes beyond. In
marked contrast to the blighted acreage which characterises the farm, the
wetlands seen from here are rich in plantlife. To the north, the ruined fields
stretch for some distance, whilst just west of here a small cottage can be seen,
its once smartly white-washed wooden walls now soiled and rotting.
name: Beside an Old Barn

desc:
South of here the trail disappears completely, as it heads into the marshes, but
to the east it seems to have found a patch of fairly solid ground. Beyond a
broken gate to the north, a stony track overgrown with weeds leads leads toward
a wooden cottage and a large old barn.
name: On a Trail through the Marshland

desc:
Running from east to west here, the trail has found fairly solid ground, as it
passes by an old cottage, standing to the north. Though perhaps at one time the
building was a pleasant one, it now has a run-down air about it, and the roses
which once climbed its southern wall have been allowed to wither and die.
name: Picking a Trail through the Marshes

desc:
Meandering idly through these lowlands, the Hoarwell sweeps past from north to
west here, its waters rising and falling with the passing seasons to feed the
mashes. To the north the ground appears to be a little more firm, and a narrow
path makes its way around the edge of the fens. Beyond this, a small stone
building can be seen, with a thin whisp of black smoked twisting up from its
chimney.
name: Far into the Marshes

desc:
West and north the dismal marshland stretches into the distance, but it is
possible to travel in those directions by picking a route carefully between the
worst patches of mire. The same cannot be said of the land to the south, which
is so boggy as to be impassable. Eastward the ground meets a slight rise, and
becomes comparatively dry.
name: Far into the Marshes

desc:
An old wooden ladder, most of its rungs now missing, is still fastened to the
wall of the barn, but now it climbs without purpose. The floor of the hayloft
lies scattered, haphazard piles of fallen planks covering the dusty floor of the
building, the walls of the animal stalls seem to have been demolished on
purpose, and someone has shattered the water troughs into smithereens.
name: In a Dusty Barn

desc:
Though the woodland is sparse, the trees scattered quite far apart, this place
still has a slightly unwholesome and gloomy air. The timbers are tall, but
somehow sickly, their bark peeling and scarred with patches of black fungus.
Stunted gorse bushes and thin bracken seem to struggle for life, and even the
thin clumps of coarse grass seems unable to thrive.
name: Gloomy Woods

desc:
A small ring of stones stands in the centre of a small clearing, which has
obviously served as an encampment at some time in the past. South and west the
woodland rapidly thins, giving way to open plains, but to the east and north
huge, gnarled trees close in around the smaller, younger trees at the edge of
the woods. To the north the undergrowth is thick and completely impassable.
Eastward it seems less dense, though it is hard to see a path through it toward
the centre of the woods.
name: At an Old Encampment

desc:
Small, thick gorse bushes grow all about, with dark green bracken, coarse
grasses, and patches of heather carpeting the ground between them. In the lee of
a small, scattered wood, and fed by the bubbling spring, this part of the plain
is much richer in vegetation than the bleak, stony land just south of here.
name: In the Eaves of a Small Wood

desc:
The bleak and stony plain opens out to the south, with only a few sparse,
yellowing clumps of grass to cover the thin soil. But to the east, west and
north, stubby gorse bushes grow in abundance, their thick and twining roots
clinging tenaciously to the rocky ground.
name: Bushy Plains

desc:
Though still quite stony, the ground here seems a little more rich than it is to
the south. Covering the area in a thick tangle of branches, small yet dense
gorse bushes abound, with dark green leaves and sharp thorns. East and north the
brush thickens considerably.
name: Bushy Plains

desc:
Dark, dry clumps of earth make the ground uneven and awkward to move over. The
area is totally dominated by tall, thorny bushes growing so close together in
places that it looks like a thorny wall. Inbetween the bushes dry grass covers
the ground. This uninviting terrain goes on in all directions, and passage east
and south is impossible due to the density of the shrubs and bushes.
name: Dense Brush

desc:
The dreary brush goes on in all directions consisting of short green grass and
three feet high bushes. The shrubbery is so dense to the east that passage is
impossible. The leaves have sharp needle-like thorns that tear your skin even
through clothes and thin leather.
name: Dense Brush

desc:
The only vegetation that has found room to grow here is short green grass and
three feet high bushes. The ground seems fertile enough but this plant life is
able to totally dominate. The brush is so dense to the east that passage is
impossible while it is possible to go north and south with difficulty.
name: Edge of the Brush

desc:
These grassy plains continue east and west for many miles into the distance,
becoming more rugged in the east. South, you can see the old hard-packed dirt
road that runs from Bree to Tharbad. The plains continue north, getting more and
more brushy.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
Harsh weather has eroded this flat, exposed land and depleted most of its
topsoil over the years. Now all that remains is a thin covering of stony dirt
unable to support more than the most feeble of plant life. A few rough patches
of grass cling hopelessly to the rough ground, but no plant any more substantial
seems able to survive here.
name: Rugged Plains

desc:
A long, narrow ditch runs parallel to the road, which is just south of here. For
most of its length, it is filled with brambles. The ground is quite muddy, and
the sides of the ditch fairly steep, but roots poke through the soft, damp
earth, making it easy enough to scramble out, and just west of here it becomes
increasingly shallow, as it approaches a sharp turn in the thoroughfare.
name: In a Ditch beside the Road

desc:
Bending from south to west, this culvert faithfully tracks the rambling route
followed by the road to Tharbad. At its deepest here, the bottom of the trench
is extremely muddy, making each step treacherously slippery, and threatening to
tumble travellers into the sharp, thorny brambles.
name: Along the Muddy Ditch

desc:
Not far to the east, a faint gravelly trail can be seen, crossing this low
plateau, and turning north, toward a thin tract of scattered wooland. At the
edge of the plateau, to the west, the ground dips down into a deep ditch, before
rising again to meet the roadway, which cuts a massive scar across the
countryside.
name: On a Low Plateau

desc:
There is a fine line between the woodland to the northeast and the plains to the
south and west. The vegetation here is sparse, but the grasses and flowers grow
richer than they do on the more exposed portions of the plateau, where the thin,
stony ground seems unable to support much life. The Road to Tharbad can be seen,
cutting a scar across the countryside, to the southwest.
name: Near the Border of a Small Wood

desc:
Only a sparse covering of limp grasses and a few stunted briers seem able to
survive in this harsh environment, depleted as it is of all but a thin layer of
gravelly dirt. However, approaching the slight shelter offered by a thin tract
of woodland the vegetation becomes more abundant further east.
name: Rugged Plains

desc:
A faint trail across the plateau deadends here, as it meets the borders of a
small, but surprisingly dense woodland. In the shelter of trees which stand to
the north, the vegetation here is denser then it is on those parts of the
plateau lying further to the south, and the soil deeper and richer.
name: On the Edge of a Small Woodland

desc:
An old stone wall stands to the east. Whilst crumbling, and in need of repair in
many places, it is nevertheless too tall to be scaled with any ease. North and
westward a brooding woodland rears up, casting quite an unwholesome air over the
surrounding land. Although not extensive, it is dense and dark, and indeed
progress west into its heart is made impossible by its tangled undergrowth.
name: At the Eaves of a Gloomy Woodland

desc:
This tiny hill is nevertheless one of the highest points for many miles. Most of
the surrounding countryside is comprised of plains and marshland, though a low
plateau clims away from the river basin, to the southwest, and just not far in
the distance, to the north a small, but dense wood can be spied.
name: Atop a Small Hill

desc:
In the lee of a small, scattered woodland, this small hollow offers a
surprisingly sheltered location. It is nestled between a low hill to the east,
and the gentle slope westward, which rises toward a massive plateau, standing
just a little above the level of the surrounding countryside. Fed by drainage
from the higher plateau, this hollow is lush. Thick grass and wildflowers grow
in profusion, in a niche whose beauty and peace liken it to a high mountain
meadow.
name: In a Wide Grassy Hollow

desc:
The plateau reaches an end here, almost imperceptibly, as the land begins to
fall off gently toward a soft grassy hollow to the east. Westward, a faint stony
trail cuts its way across the flat lands, often almost losing itself amid the
stubby grasses, short weeds, and rough, stony terrain.
name: At the Edge of the Plateau

desc:
A faint trail can be seen crossing the thin grasses of the plateau, and making
its way toward a small woodland north of here. Rapid drainage from this area,
toward the rivers and marshes below, means that the land can only support weak
vegetation, and much of the soil has washed away over the years, leaving only a
thin covering of stony dirt.
name: Travelling a Faint Trail across the Plateau

desc:
A shallow but extremely muddy ditch extends to the north, but it comes to an
abrupt end just south of here where the roadway makes another turn. Tough
brambles make progress along the trench painfully difficult, and the sludgy
ground offers little purchase. However, here there is less risk of exposure to
unfriendly eyes than on the road or open plains.
name: In a Muddy Ditch

desc:
The wide plains stretch on, spreading out to cover most of the landscape east of
here. Not far to the north the grasses give way, and eventually surrender their
claim of the terrain to a small woodland. To the south, at the bottom of a
grassy bank, the Road to Tharbad meanders across the countryside.
name: On a Low Plateau

desc:
A faint path tracks its way across the plateau here, sweeping from the east to
the north, approaching a small, but dense woodland. To the south and west a wide
road cuts through the countryside. A few small wildflowers, and a thin blanket
of yellwing grasses borders the path, but this part of the plataeu is not at all
rich in vegetation, and the ground is quite stony.
name: On a Low Plateau

desc:
A faint, stony trail cuts its way through the thin grasses, heading from the
west, towards the edge of the plateau, just east of here. Below lies a wide
river basin, extending eastward across marshes and wetland, towards the mighty
rivers Hoarwell and Glanduin, which come to a confluence in this region, and
flow on, southwest toward Lond Daer and the coast.
name: On a Low Plateau

desc:
The escarpment becomes quite steep here, but the rise is fairly short, and the
plateau is still not much higher than the plains below. Lush grass grows on this
embankment, and is fed by drainage from the upland in the lowlands to the east
and also from the waters of the river, further east.
name: On a Steep Escarpment

desc:
Richly watered by the runoff from the low plateau to the west and the small hill
just north of here, this area of the plains is particularly verdant. The grass
grows long and thick, but even so, wildflowers and small shrubs abound. To the
east a small cottage can be seen, and south from this spot the plain stretches
onward until it meets the fens.
name: Plain before a Low Hill

desc:
A narrow path plods through the marshes to the east and south, but here the land
is characterized by long, lush green grass. This rich meadow is bordered some
distance to the north by a gently sloping hill, and far to the west a low
plateau watches over the grassland.
name: At the Edge of the Lush Plains

desc:
North and west a steep grassy escarpment rears up toward a low-lying plateau. To
the east, the flourishing grassland stretches off into the distance and
eventually meets the creeping marshland, whose borders seem to crawl further
into the plains each year. Thick, dark green grass sways in a light breeze,
stretching south as far as the eye can see.
name: Amid the Grasses of a Lush Plain

desc:
This steep bank is covered with thick grass, enriched by the drainage of water
from the low plateau above. West and north you may reach the top of the slope,
and the escarpment stretches on to the south, before sweeping westward toward a
wide road.
name: On a Steep, Grassy Escarpment

desc:
A wide plain opens out to the north, covered by sparse grass and a scattering of
tiny wildflowers. Though the lands to the east are lush, the plains atop the
plateau show much poorer vegatation, as the waters required for life are rapidly
drained from here, toward the rivers and marshes below.
name: On a Low Plateau

desc:
Only a short distance from the road to Tharbad, the land begins to climb gently,
a long grassy slope, rising toward a low plateau. The bank is pock- marked by
innumerable small burrows, many of which are at least partially hidden by the
long, dark green blades, and tiny flowers are scattered like jewels among the
lush vegetation.
name: On a Grassy Bank

desc:
The road travels here on a slightly raised causeway. A little below the road, a
narrow path stumbles around the western edge of the fen, in places totally
consumed by oozing mud. Marshland stretches all the way to the slow-moving river
in the east and extends to the city wall in the south.
name: On a Path by the Marshes

desc:
Stretching onward to the north, flat as far as the eye can see, the marshes make
a singularly uninviting prospect. Brown, murky water puddles here and there, and
the ground has a spongy feel underfoot. To the east and south the Hoarwell
meanders lazily by, feeding the fens with its burgeoning waters,but to the west
a narrow path struggles for existence, against the ever-widening quagmire.
name: Rank Marshland

desc:
The River Greyflood, which flows not far to the east of here, makes its presence
felt by creeping across the flat, low-lying land, making it into a wide marsh. A
narrow trail used to pass through this area, but now it is almost lost in the
thick, oozing mud and foetid pools of murky brown water.
name: In the Mire

desc:
A stench of rotting matter fills the air here, and the insistent drone of
