insects buzzes all around. The ground feels spongy and wet underfoot, and small
pools of murky water collect here and there. The only vegetation which seems
able to survive in this festering marshland is an occasional tuft of coarse,
yellowing sedge.
name: A Festering Quagmire

desc:
Standing on comparatively solid ground here, the path draws close to the remains
of a huge and solitary tree. Now but a lifeless husk, it was split asunder,
apparently long ago, perhaps by a bolt of lightning. Nevertheless its colossal
roots anchor this patch of ground against the encroaching marsh.
name: On a Path by the Marshes

desc:
Soft green grass thickly covers the ground all around, and tiny wildflowers grow
amid this lush vegetation. But not far to the east, a wide marshland is slowly
encroaching on these low-lying plains, which stand at the borders of the Road to
Tharbad.
name: Lush Plains

desc:
This tall, grassy bank climbs toward a low plateau, which overlooks the river
basin. Steepening, and growing taller to the north, the escarpment becomes
little more than a grassy bank as it sweeps west, towards the road. To the east
lie lush plains, fed by the waters draining from the upland, and bordering a
wide marshland, which extends toward the distant river.
name: On a Steepening Escarpment

desc:
Sweeping round to the east, the grassy bank is swiftly becoming rather taller,
and longer, but maintaining a gentle slope, as the plateau seems to climb ever
higher above the elevation of the plains below. However, this effect is only an
illusion, and this remains predominantly a lowland area, with the plains
actually dipping down toward the river, rather than the plateau rising.
name: On a Soft Grassy Bank

desc:
Tall, dark green grass sways in the lightest breeze, looking like nothing so
much as a rolling green ocean. This verdant land is fed by the waters of the
Hoarwell and Glanduin, which often break their banks and flood vast areas of the
river basin. To the west an escarpment climbs to a low, exposed plain.
name: Amid the Grasses of a Lush Plain

desc:
The marshes are slowly encroaching on on this narrow path, which seems to be
little used. East and south the mire seems fairly passable, albeit only with
care. Nevertheless, this seems to be the direction taken by this trail. The
ground slopes gently upward to the west and north, and this slightly higher
elevation has spared the grassland there, at least for now, from the creeping
waters of the river.
name: On a Path at the Edge of the Marshes

desc:
At the centre of a huge clearing in the woods stands a massive tree, which
reaches high into the sky, its branches forming a living roof above your head.
The trunk of the tree is more than eight feet in diameter, and covered with
thick, dark brown bark. Small flowers of red, blue and yellow fill the clearing
and dance and sway in a light breeze.
name: At a Huge Tree

desc:
South and west the undergrowth forms a dense tangle beneath the trees, and is
quite impenetrable. At the centre of the clearing stands a massive tree, with a
trunk whose girth is as large as a small building. Northward, the clearing
stretches toward a more open part of the woods, and yet the trees there seem
gloomy and brooding, unwholesome, and somehow tainted.
name: At the Edge of a Clearing

desc:
A small wooden cottage, its once white facade now stained with dust and dirt,
stands just east of here. A gravelly path, quite overgrown with weeds, leads
from the threshold, and heads south, toward a trail, which skirts along the edge
of a broad expanse of marshland. To the north a large barn can be seen, its old
wooden doors warped and twisted by the ravages of the weather, and a lack of
maintenance. However, despite all these signs of apparent ruin and abandonment,
a thin wisp of smoke tails from the chimney of the cottage, suggesting that
someone must still occupy the building.
name: By the Neglected Cottage

desc:
Heavy with the scents of mold and rot, stale air fills this small room. The
floorboards have broken in several locations from years of strain, creating
gaping, black holes in the floor. A small table and a chair, both of which seem
to be about to collapse, stand where the holes in the floor are less numerous.
You can see little else in the room, apart from a small fireplace occupying the
north-west corner of the room. A simple door in the west wall leads to the fresh
air outside, and a doorway leads north into another room.
name: In the Small Cottage

desc:
This claustrophobically small bedroom is shrouded in constant gloom, as the
windows have been barricaded many years ago. A small couch stands by the
northern wall, its sheets grey with dirt, while in the middle of the room, an
aged bottle with a candle stuck into it stands, apparently used to chase away at
least a fraction of the shadows that fill the room. Animal bones and shreds of
cloth litter the floor, adding to the general mess.
name: The Messy Bedroom

desc:
Some remains of a few fish lie scattered in the shallow water. Apparently some
animal had lunch here. Maybe this would be a good place for a hungry hunter to
catch a meal.
name: Glanduin Delta

desc:
Bordered by a grassy bank to the east, and a spongy path to the north, this
small bit of the marshes is isolated from most of the rest of the Swanfleet
Marshes. Looking southwards you see islands of golden foliage amidst the crystal
clear waters of the river Glanduin.
name: Swanfleet Marshes

desc:
The ground here is damp and covered with spongy, green grass. To the east a
slight crest rises noticeably out of the surrounding marshland, its peak hidden
by a crown of dense, tangled foliage. You catch a glimpse of some sort of metal
structure on the hill, but cannot reach it from this direction. To the west a
peaceful marsh exists where the Glanduin and Mitheithel meet, and to the north
you see a junction of paths. To the southeast, a faint odour of must and decay
breaks the otherwise tranquil environment.
name: Bank of the River Glanduin

desc:
Rising here and there from the shallow water are thick, brownish straws of
grass. The water runs calmly here, filling your ears with a soothing refreshing
sound. The Glanduin continues to the west and south.
name: The Glanduin

desc:
A narrow trail, surrounded by reeds, leads away from the shady riverbank to the
east, its path marked by the trampled grass that lies around you. The damp soil
makes it hard to continue on your way. A small hill is visible in the south.
name: Small Path

desc:
In another time, this must have been a frequently used path, but somehow it came
to be abandoned. The ground here is more dry, and continues into the golden
plains to the east. The path leads onward to the south.
name: Small Path

desc:
The grass here is tall and golden, and sways gently in the breeze. This vast
ocean of moving colour seems to continue endlessly to the south and east. To the
west you spot a small path trampled into the thick grass, and a barely
noticeable sound of rushing water comes from that direction.
name: Golden Plains

desc:
This part of the road seems to have sunk a bit lower than the rest. Water from
several stagnant pools makes travel on the road treacherous. A flooded field
lies to the south, while eastwards the land begins to dry up into grassy plains.
The road continues on to the north and west.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
The feature that draws the most attention in this area is a dark, ominous pool.
It does not appear to be very deep, but the muddy water makes the approximate
depth hard to tell. Murky water pours into the pool from the swamps to both the
north and west. A stretch of an old road can be seen from here to the south.
name: A Large Stinking Pool

desc:
Bushes and small thickets of magnolias grow out of the wet ground. The bushes
become much thicker to the north and east. Behind these bushes, some sort of
tower can be seen. A horrible stench draws attention to an ominous, murky pool
to the south.
name: In the Swamps

desc:
This stretch of the road is pitted with potholes filled with muddy water. West
and north of here are more of the swamps that dominate the region. A large pool
of water to the north collects water from the surrounding area. The road
continues on to the east and south.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
This section of the road seems fairly firm and stable, as compared to the west
where the swamp has begun to overtake the roadway. To the north is the beginning
of a swampy area. To the south of the road some plains can be seen. The road
continues on to the east and west.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
The road is slighty damp and muddy, with occasional stones poking out of the
ground. Beds of magnolias mark the outlines of the road, which continues on
towards the north and west. To the south and east the flowers and grasses begin
to merge with thicker vegetation.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
The road is barely visible because of the weeds and thick grass that have grown
over it. Small pine trees and beds of magnolias barely outline the edges of the
road. The road appears to continue to the south and east. Dense thickets block
passage to the north, while to the west the weeds and grasses begin to merge
with the outskirts of a swamp.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
This wet path heads to the west, slightly covered with grass and twigs. The
golden grass surrounds it to the north and south. The cold wind would make the
way very uncomfortable, lashing the dress of some weary traveller in this open
flat.
name: Path through the Wetlands

desc:
Here the path leads west, deeper into the marshes. The soil seems to be less
damp to the east, and indeed some green healthy grass is visible in that
direction. The thick vegetation here makes it hard to continue on your way.
name: Path through the Wetlands

desc:
The ground here is extremely moist, and the thick foliage surrounding you grows
taller than a man. A traveller's feet would sink deeply into the murky water,
chilling his skin and slowing his pace.
name: Path through the Wetlands

desc:
The trail of trampled weeds and marsh-grass continues to the east. To the west
the trail is bordered by a wet field, which runs up to a grassy riverbank,
beyond which the regal Hoarwell can be seen. To your south the path leads into
an exceedingly wet section of marshland.
name: Small Path

desc:
The path here bends westward, towards some grassy wetlands. The ever present
sound of running water is stronger from that direction. To the south, the dense
vegetation is too thick and tangled to continue.
name: Bend in the Small Path

desc:
The path here runs in a north-south direction, bordered on the west by a slope,
and on the east by a vast expanse of rolling fields. The westward hill looks as
if it would be an ideal place to take in the surroundings from.
name: Small Path

desc:
You are at the top of a small hill, surrounded by flat, grassy land to the east
and the river Hoarwell to the west. The high altitude offers a panoramic view of
the region. To the south-west two rivers seem to come together. Beyond that the
magnificent towers and steep walls of Tharbad loom on the horizon.
name: Overlooking the Hoarwell

desc:
The golden, rolling fields spread out in all directions but west, where you can
see a small path beaten into the thick grass. You can barely make out the sound
of rushing water from that direction, although a steep hill prevents you from
seeing its source.
name: Golden Plains

desc:
The vast golden plains seem to roll endlessly into the eastern horizon. The
ground here is dry but fertile, supporting a healthy crop of thick ground
foliage. To the west and south a small path leads away, and to the north and
east the fields continue with no landmarks, so that a traveller could easily get
lost.
name: Golden Plains

desc:
The river flows slightly slower in this section, allowing a decent view of the
muddy bank to the south. Past the bank the land appears to gradually fade into
swamps and fens. The Glanduin River continues its way east and west. Every once
in awhile a small fish can be seen swimming along the murky riverbed.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
The current of the river is markedly slower here, as it widens and enters an
area of majestic marshland. The Glanduin continues to the north, and to the east
the water is shallow enough for a man to stand. The peaceful sounds of wildlife
drift on the gentle breeze, as small wetland birds and creatures go about their
business.
name: The Glanduin

desc:
The current of the Glanduin is more relaxed as the river opens into the
Swanfleet marshes, also known as Nin-in-Eilph. To the north the marshes seem to
be darker and thicker than anywhere else, restricting any further progress in
that direction. Beyond these dim waters a densely vegetated hillock rises, a
curious site amidst the otherwise flat landscape.
name: The Glanduin

desc:
The river Glanduin flows all around you, moving slowly off to the west where it
forms a large delta in the distance. The water is brown and unclear, making it
hard to discern the bottom, despite the water being quite shallow. To the north
the Swanfleet marshes begin.
name: The Glanduin

desc:
The river flows swiftly here, fed by icy springs deep within the distant Misty
Mountains. To the northeast you see a thick, dim marsh that makes travel in this
direction almost impossible, beyond which you can vaguely discern rolling
grassland. To the west the current slows and the river appears to branch into a
broad delta.
name: The Glanduin

desc:
The current here almost slows down to a standstill. The water to the south
becomes murky as it thickens into a vast, swampy area. The other edge of the
Glanduin can be seen to the north. The river continues on towards the east and
west in almost a perfect line.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
The river flows swiftly as it flows from the west towards a lake towards the
east. To the southeast, past the muddy banks, a tower can be seen above the
foliage of the swamps. An occasional fish can be seen swimming along the bottom
of the river, and several swans have taken refuge by the south river bank. The
water has a brownish tinge as mud from the swamps to the south merge with the
river.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
The river flows lazily toward the west. The Glanduin is not known especially for
its strong current. It is wide enough to make travel uncomfortable without a
boat.
name: The Glanduin

desc:
The river Glanduin runs westwards through the marshes which rise up on the north
side of the bank. The air is still and quiet, punctuated only by an occasional
low howl from the marshes.
name: The Glanduin

desc:
The faint outlines of an overgrown path can be seen here leading to the south.
Old weathered stones serve as markers for the path. An old elven road passes by
to the north and west. To the east is a grass covered plain. An occasional bird
can be seen flying overhead.
name: Beginning of the Faint Path

desc:
Thick grass muffles the sounds of the area, making the place very silent. Only
some occasional rocks and thickets break up the rolling landscape of the plains.
A road can be spied to the north, but otherwise the plains seem to spread out in
all directions.
name: Large Plain

desc:
A large swampy area inhabits most of this area. Most of the land is covered by
thick, soft mud or watery pools. Sprouting from the mud are various brown
grasses and tall weeds. The vegetation becomes too thick towards the north,
making passage in that direction impossible.
name: Outskirts of the Swamp

desc:
The bushes in this area have grown together very close. The vegetation becomes
extremely thick to the north and west, making travel in those directions quite
impossible. The ground is very soft and wet, often ankle deep mud in places.
Brackish water collects in pools, attracting many flying insects.
name: Muddy Bushes

desc:
This area is dominated by muddy soil covered in thick brown grass. The ground is
very unstable, making travel difficult. An old road can be spied rising above
the land to the east. The grasses merge with dense bushes as the plains continue
to the south and west. Far off in the distance to the north is the river
Glanduin. A tower overlooks the surrounding area to the north east.
name: Dense Grass

desc:
The ground is soft and wet, with occasionally pools of murky water. There are
many bushes growing in the area. Many are harmless, but some have sharp leaves
or thorns that could slash unprotected skin. The bushes continue on towards the
west, but begin to fade towards the north and east. Passage to the south becomes
difficult as the vegetation becomes even thicker in that direction, making
travel hard at best.
name: Bushes

desc:
While an occasional scraggly bush pushes itself out of the tough soil, most of
the ground is covered with pools of stagnant, muddy water. The pools gradually
merge to the north into a wide river. The pools continue on to the west, but the
ground becomes somewhat firmer to the south. Dense bushes hamper travels to the
east.
name: Muddy Pools

desc:
The vegetation is very scarce here. Tufts of hard, short grass grow out of the
river banks. The river continues to flow past to the north. The grounds to the
west and southwest slowly turn into a wasteland of bubbling mud, slender reeds,
and buzzing insects.
name: Muddy Banks

desc:
The ground here is quite treacherous, consisting of both hard and soft mud.
Sinkholes threaten to capture unwary travellers, making the journey through this
region slow and tedious. The ground between these mudholes is overgrown by dense
grass and small bushes. The mud and grass continues on to the west. The rustling
of the river Glanduin can be heard to the north. The ground hardens to the
south, making travelling easier. Some ruins can be spied to the east through the
vegetation.
name: Overgrown River Banks

desc:
The swamps here are full of murky pools and mudholes. Tall reeds grow out of the
ground. The pools begin to increase in size and finally merge into a river to
the north. The swamps continue on towards the south and west. A large building
is to the east, but cannot be reached from this side.
name: Swampy River Banks

desc:
You have entered a decaying building. It has obviously not been inhabited for a
very long time. The wooden parts are mostly gone and there is no sign of
furniture. There is a big arch in the east wall, the only thing left of the
doors that once were. The floor is made of stones and is littered by broken
stone blocks and dirt from the outside which the wind has brought with it. At
the west side of the room stands an old staircase made of a dark coloured wood.
It once led up to the crumbling tower that rises above you. The staircase is in
really bad shape and the top part is missing.
name: Decaying Building

desc:
Your steps are very insecure here, but the tower seems more stable than it
looked like from below. It is only about twenty feet high, but there are no big
obstacles around to obstruct the view. Just north of here the river Glanduin
flows from east to west. Further to the east lies a wide lake. On the other bank
of the river there is a hill and to the west a swamp stretches as far as you can
see. The horizon is flat to the south but far to the east, veiled in mists you
more guess than see the outline of a mountain range. This is an ideal place for
watching the traffic on the river.
name: Crumbling Tower

desc:
You see a beautiful natural vale hidden from its surroundings by the sheer sides
of the crater that it lies in. Due to frequent flooding of the Glanduin this
vale turned into a small lake.
name: Small Lake

desc:
The vegetation around you becomes denser as you approach the end of the
Swanfleet marshes. To the west a path is barely visible, and a small lake is
situated to the north.
name: Swanfleet Marshes

desc:
The soil is damp here, it seems like it is often flooded by the waters of the
Glanduin. The Swanfleet marshes continue to the north and east. To the south is
a marshy rise beyond which you can hear the gentle flow of the River Glanduin.
name: Swanfleet Marshes

desc:
Rising above the calm surface of the surrounding water, this small island is
covered with thick, green grass. It is evident from the myriad nests and
scattered remains that this isolated crest is home for many species of wetland
bird.
name: Marshy Rise

desc:
The thick and dim marsh of the Swanfleet surrounds you on all sides. The soil
looks brown, damp and ugly, with little sign of wildlife. To the south the river
Glanduin flows on into the west, where it forms a large delta several miles
distant.
name: Swanfleet Marshes

desc:
Much of the open water here is covered with green aquatic plants that hide the
murky depths beneath. The wet and soft soil gives under the slightest weight. To
the south the waters of the Glanduin flow slowly to the west, where they join
the other mighty river, the Hoarwell.
name: Swanfleet Marshes

desc:
This part of the marshes looks a bit more peaceful. One can hear the sounds of
wildlife here and there, but always in the distance, never close by. The marshes
continue to the west and south, and to the east and north the wind roams in the
open plains, covered by the green and golden grass.
name: Swanfleet Marshes

desc:
The ground here is moist and fertile, sprouting many grasses and small flowers.
The sounds of marshy fauna reach your ears from the south, but otherwise not a
sound of life comes from the marsh in the west.
name: Golden Plains

desc:
Stepping away from the path into the dense reeds, your feet sink quickly into
the thick mud. Grasping for a handhold, you find only tangled foliage that tears
easily from the ground, offering no support. You are overcome by the smell of
rotting plant life and stagnant mud.
name: Dangerous Marsh

desc:
Stretching out to the horizon in all directions, you can only see these marshes.
It looks especially thick and dangerous to the north. The dank smell of decay
comes from that direction.
name: Swanfleet Marshes

desc:
Stretching out to the horizon in all directions, you can only see these marshes.
For the inexperienced traveller it would be hard not to get lost in here. The
smell of rotten vegetation is thick in the air.
name: Swanfleet Marshes

desc:
The odour of marsh gas and rotten vegetation is prominent, and the smell seems
to cling to everything. In all directions you can see these mighty marshes,
except east where you can barely make out the distinct smell of sweet, fresh air
blowing gently towards you.
name: Swanfleet Marshes

desc:
Only about eight feet above the floor of the stables, this fairly narrow loft
has bales of hay stacked two and three deep from one end to the other. Peering
over the hay down into the stables, one can see a row of wooden troughs and a
seemingly endless row of stalls. From this vantage point, one can also clearly
see all three entrances to the stables. Also, three narrow slits on the south,
east, and western walls provide a good view of the land for several miles.
name: A Well-Stocked Hay Loft

desc:
Spacious, yet draughty and quite smelly, this large wooden building has stalls
for about two dozen steeds. Hay conceals the earthen floor of the stables plus
the base of a large wooden trough in the centre of the room. A few barrels are
piled against the northern wall next to a wide, covered pathway leading toward
the tavern. Almost curiously, vines from the trellises of the passage climb up
the walls and twist their way along the beams and rafters of the high ceiling,
past the loft on the southern wall and outside through the three open windows.
name: Inside the Stables

desc:
Meticulously crafted from thin, delicate-looking wooden slats, the latticework
overhead is covered with thick, winding vines. From the tavern on the northern
side to the stables in the south, the lush, green espalier gently flows across
the trellises, sometimes spilling onto the nearby buildings, but often pouring
down the walls to meet the grass on the edges of the path. A break in the wall
to the west reveals a small shop beyond a narrow wooden door. Right above the
door rests a white sign painted with green letters to match the flora.
name: A Covered Path

desc:
Wide, squatty windows placed right under the ceiling make the room seem airy.
The wooden walls are decorated with intricately carved panels with different
motifs, some obviously of foreign origin. Tall racks packed to their limits with
various clothing stand along the south wall. Behind the counter in the northwest
corner, there is a closed wooden cabinet, and several shelves with various
miscellaneous objects are situated in the west part of the shop.
name: Gil's Thrift Shop

desc:
The tavern itself is lit dimly by four sets of candles suspended from the high,
sloped wooden ceiling. A larger fifth set of candles located in the centre of
the room appears to have given its last flame. As a result, the corners of the
tavern are much brighter than the middle where most of the customers sit,
talking loudly and enjoying their meals. Just west of the bar serving food and
drinks in the northeastern corner is a short corridor leading to what appears to
be an inn. Opposite the corridor a long covered passageway heads south through
an empty doorframe. Small torches reflect on the smooth, polished wooden floor
and frame the sturdy metal doors on the east and western walls.
name: Jasper's Tavern

desc:
This is a dark natural cave, carved by wind and water acting on the softer rock
beneath the harder layer that forms the surface of the hill. A pungent smell
tells you that animals of some kind have made use of this shelter.
name: An Animal's Lair

desc:
Little rain has apparently fallen here lately, and the grass grows on the dry
soil in stiff brown clumps, nibbled short by grazing animals. A road can be seen
to the east, in the distance; and a couple miles to the north can be seen a
building of some kind. A muddy slough just north of here makes it hard to go in
that direction.
name: Dry Plains

desc:
The road makes another bend here,leaving you with the options of following the
road north or east. West there are some plains. To the south you can see
something that looks like a high tower of some kind.
name: Old South Road

desc:
You find yourself inside a small outpost with a single door in the north wall.
There are some shelves and tables here. On the table there are a few plates with
some disgusting leftovers. The floor is not dusty so there has to be someone who
goes here regularly.
name: Outpost

desc:
Perhaps this was once a much-used path, but now it is cracked and over- grown
with weeds. Loose stones are scattered about, and it is hard to tell for certain
where the path ends, and the garden begins. And the garden itself, lying to
either side of the trail, is also choked with rough weeds, apparently not tended
to in many months. Even the barn to the north, to which the pathway leads,
stands in a state of disrepair.
name: On an Overgrown Pathway

desc:
The waters of the Glanduin have made a kind of flooded valley here. The land is
not overused by the small animals here and is not very comfortable to walk
across.
name: Glanduin Delta

desc:
This area of the valley is much more peaceful than the expanses of lush
vegetation filled with excited birds and other wildlife. The river is almost
still here. The water seems to be used by animals for refreshment and it is
perfectly clear and looks quite safe to drink.
name: Glanduin Delta

desc:
The river Hoarwell flows under a worn wooden bridge, that casts a thick shade on
the water. The river itself flows from the north to the south at a steady pace
and the sounds of two great rivers mixing their waters comes from the east.
name: Under the Bridge across the Hoarwell

desc:
A small circular chamber occupies the centre of the treetrunk, but leaves enough
living wood to keep the massive tree healthy and strong. The ground is soft and
mossy, and the air has a surprisingly wholesome feeling to it. High above you
the walls of this living chamber grow closer together, and eventually meet,
forming a domed roof of heartwood.
name: Inside the Tree Trunk

desc:
Thick brown roots poke through the walls of this small cavern, twisting and
turning, and forming a dense tangle, which supports the earthy walls and roof. A
sticky web bisects the chamber, and tiny beads of moisture hang from its silvery
threads, like miniature diamonds.
name: Below the Huge Tree

desc:
Whilst eastward there is a narrow, winding path between the huge, gnarled trees,
the trail dead-ends to the west, where the undergrowth becomes extremely dense.
The sickly brush and scrub here are choked by moss and fungus, as if the wood
were trying to consume itself in its sickness. The lower branches of the trees
are bare of leaves, and their bark is peeling, as if afflicted by disease.
name: Gloomy Woods

desc:
The trees crowd in on one-another here, with dense tangles of withered branches,
and sickly shrubs making the area almost impassable to the east and to the
north. The ground is spongy and damp, made slippery with foul- smelling mosses
and fungus. However, to the south, the trees open up into a surprisingly
wholesome clearing.
name: At the Heart of the Woods

desc:
Short, thorny bushes add to the otherwise awfully barren scenery. Beneath these
dark green shrubs, scattered clumps of light green, long bladed grass seem to
thrive above the shorter, darker grass that lives without this kind of
competition south of here. While the bushes are sparse enough east and west, the
thicket north of here looks impenetrable.
name: Bushy Grassland

desc:
The sound of flowing water drenches almost every other sound. This offers a
sense of security, since no one will be able to hear your doings from here
unless you make a very loud noise. But then again, hearing approaching enemies
would be extremely difficult too.
name: Marsh
